title: Scotch broth
subtitle: Mutton, pearl barley, and root vegetables
excerpt: A thick, warming broth of mutton or lamb neck, pearl barley, and winter root vegetables. One of the oldest soups in the Scottish canon and one of the most sustaining.
sourceNotes: Scotch broth is one of the oldest documented soups in Scotland. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes 'Scotch Mutton Broth' as a standard recipe. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), documents the traditional pearl barley and root vegetable version and notes its appearance in Robert Burns's 'Address to a Haggis' and in general Scottish literary tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The barley will continue to absorb liquid after freezing; add extra stock when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes a large pot that keeps in the fridge for 4 days. Better the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made a day ahead. The fat can be skimmed easily after a night in the fridge, and the flavour deepens overnight.

content.text: Scotch broth is a soup built around time. The mutton or lamb neck needs two hours at a gentle simmer to give up its flavour and its collagen to the stock; the vegetables and barley go in for the last hour. At the end you have a broth that is thick from the barley, rich from the long-cooked meat, and warming in the way that only a pot made over hours can be.

Mutton is the traditional choice and worth seeking out from a good butcher: it has more flavour than lamb neck. If mutton is unavailable, lamb neck makes an excellent broth. The meat can be shredded and returned to the pot or served on the side; in the traditional version it goes back into the soup in large, rustic pieces.

items.prepNote: stalks and leaves separated; stalks tied for the pot, leaves chopped to finish

content.text: Place the mutton or lamb pieces in a large stockpot and cover with {{water}} of cold water. Bring to the boil, then skim the grey foam that rises to the surface over the first 5 to 10 minutes. This step is worth taking time over: a well-skimmed pot produces a clearer, cleaner-tasting broth. Add the onion, celery, parsley stalks, and bay leaves. Season with salt.

Partially cover and simmer gently over low heat for 2 hours. The liquid should tremble, not boil. Remove the meat pieces and set aside to cool slightly. Discard the onion, celery, parsley stalks, and bay leaves.

Add {{pearl-barley}} of rinsed pearl barley, {{carrot}} diced carrots, {{turnip}} diced turnip, and {{leek}} sliced leeks to the broth. Cook over medium-low heat for 40 to 45 minutes until the barley is swollen and tender and the vegetables are soft. Meanwhile, remove the mutton meat from the bones and tear into large pieces, discarding the bones and any gristle. Return the meat to the pot.
Taste and adjust the seasoning. The broth will have thickened considerably from the barley; if it is very thick, add a ladleful of hot water. Serve scattered with the reserved chopped parsley leaves.

items.cause: Not enough skimming at the start, or the water boiled too hard
items.fix: Keep the heat low enough to maintain a gentle simmer throughout. If the broth is already cloudy, it does not affect flavour — only appearance.

items.symptom: Barley has absorbed all the liquid and the broth is very thick
items.cause: Pearl barley continues to absorb liquid after cooking
items.fix: Add hot water or stock to let down the consistency. Reseason after adding liquid.

items.symptom: Meat is still tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Heat was too high and the collagen seized rather than dissolving; or the pieces were very large
items.fix: Continue simmering over the lowest possible heat for another 30 to 45 minutes. The mutton should pull apart easily from the bone.

items.cause: Insufficient seasoning, or the meat had little inherent flavour
items.fix: Add a splash of Worcestershire sauce and taste again. Season generously with salt — barley absorbs salt during cooking.

content.text: Scotch broth is old enough that F. Marian McNeill, writing The Scots Kitchen in 1929, traces its history back to at least the mediaeval period in Scotland, when a pot of mutton and barley was the standard winter meal across much of the country. Beeton included it in 1861 as 'Scotch Mutton Broth', and the recipe she gives is recognisably the same dish. It is still the standard restorative soup in Scottish households, made when there is time and eaten over several days. The second day is widely considered better than the first.
